text: This depicts the planned approach thoracoscopically, typically done with three trocar sites, one for the camera and the remaining two operative ports.
The picture as seen through the thoracoscope.
Here you can see the pericardium being grasped, the application of the scissors and the egress of fluid under some pressure.
The entire view is magnified.
And as you can see here, there's some clot that is also removed from the pericardial space, presumably from the previous pericardiocentesis.
This is performed with a double lumen endotracheal tube in place and single ventilation of the contralateral side.
Typically, CO2 insufflation is not required, however, has been utilized at the outset of the procedure to achieve more rapid absorptive atelectasis of the lung.
The pericardium is then grasped and gradually dissected after careful identification of the phrenic nerve.
In most situations, we have resected the pericardium anterior to the phrenic nerve in its entirety
In selected cases with loculations, we have also removed posterior pericardium, posterior to the phrenic nerve.

In most situations, dissection of the pericardium can be performed sharply, at times requiring cauterization of vessels, with care being taken to elevate that pericardium up well away from the cardiac surface to prevent the induction of dysrhythmia.
Here one can identify the phrenic nerve seen more closely here running with its companion vessels.
You can see that the pericardial resection has been at least one centimeter anterior to the phrenic nerve.
Frequently, the pericardium is resected rather than being in one piece resected in two to three segments simply because it appears to be easier to do it that way at times.
You can see the application of some electrocautery here to an area of vasculature in the pericardium.
After completion of resection of the large patch of pericardium anteriorly, you can see that the entire anterior surface of the heart is well seen.
Typically, the resection of pericardium has been approximately an 8 by 8 centimeter patch or square of pericardium removed anteriorly.

After completion of the pericardial resection, a drainage catheter is left in the pericardial space, directed under direct vision thoracoscopically, and left in place until drainage is less than 150 cc over a 24-hour period of time.
The second case depicts that of a patient with a process other than an effusive pericardial problem.
We have found that the effusive problem is the
process which thoracoscopic pericardiectomy has been most useful.
It is not a procedure to be used for constrictive pericarditis.
However, this case depicts a patient with uremia and uremic pericarditis who has the bread and butter pericarditis that is frequently seen in these patients.
And this can be done thoracoscopically.
As opposed to the first case, I think you can see the much more thickened pericardium and somewhat loosely adherent to the underlying epicardial surface, but it can be dissected off fairly easily.
Whereas we have felt that a large pericardial resection is important for
for limiting the incidence of recurrent effusive pericardial disease and in addition for preventing the development of late constriction.
In the uremic patients we have found that a smaller resection more approximating that of a pericardial window has been acceptable and has worked well.
You can see here the large pericardial resection, pericardial window performed for the patient with uremia.
